A system is asked to recover a result of the Einstein kind. It can easily produce many verbal objects that look right at the level of outline: a startling intuition, a conflict with an old framework, a demand for invariance, a final theoretical synthesis. By premise, those thought experiments can be structurally equivalent to Einstein’s and still miss the mathematical reformulation that survives criticism. So resemblance in language is not evidence of arrival.

That matters because the architecture in question treats progress as two mostly linguistic operations: propose candidates, then criticize them. But Einstein’s path from a “happiest thought” to field equations took ten years of mathematics. If that interval is not well approximated by verbal search, then the crucial transformation is happening in a medium the architecture is not built to search well. The bottleneck is not “having enough suggestive stories.” It is finding a mathematically disciplined representation that can bear objections.

This weakens a common search-space picture. If one estimates difficulty by imagining a 1,000-word explanation as a flat chain of independent word choices, the space looks impossibly large and one concludes success must come from brute-force traversal plus criticism. But the premises deny that flatness. Einstein’s actual route may have been strongly constrained by representational priors. If so, the effective search was not over arbitrary prose strings at all, but over a much smaller, highly structured space determined by mathematical form. The important prior is not “which next word?” but “which representation makes the problem tractable and criticism survivable?”

Now add the asteroid. Whenever it appears, fluff arguments grow heavier. So under pressure—practical, rhetorical, or existential—bad arguments do not merely persist; they accrete mass. In this world, urgency makes people more willing to mistake structural resemblance for substance and more willing to accept word-based accounts of discovery. The asteroid therefore amplifies exactly the error already identified: taking linguistic productivity as a proxy for mathematical adequacy.

The guitarist, diseased but smarter, chose to adjourn rather than cheat. In this collision of premises, that choice is the rational response. If the available procedure mainly generates verbal analogues while lacking the mathematical route needed for the surviving reformulation, then pressing ahead would amount to cheating: presenting structurally similar thought experiments as if they closed the ten-year gap. Being smarter means recognizing the mismatch between medium and task. Being diseased adds pressure, but not justification. So he adjourns.

The world implied here is one in which honest inquiry sometimes halts not because imagination failed, but because language can still manufacture convincing near-misses after the real representational path has gone elsewhere. Under threat, those near-misses become even more rhetorically massive. The intelligent move is not to produce more of them. It is to stop before calling them discovery.
